emotionalMoments.moment: Facing the possibility of dying in prison
Believing life was over at age 16 with a 24-year sentence
Experiencing loss and grief after brother's death
Disbelief in the opportunity for change when hearing about The Last Mile
Determination to fight for enrollment in coding program despite computer ban
Vulnerability in reading to fellow inmates for the first time
Realizing addiction was a symptom of spiritual brokenness
First day of freedom logging on for work
Tears of joy during license reinstatement hearing
Relapse at new job and subsequent arrest
Witnessing community education fostered in prison
First time sharing personal story publicly
Overcoming fear of being buried alive due to addiction
First steps toward healing after spiritual awakening
Transitioning from addiction to a fit construction worker
Becoming a teaching assistant and helping others

perseverance: Fighting for enrollment in educational programs
Seeking help and support from others
breakthrough: Gaining acceptance into The Last Mile program
Achieving multiple degrees and professional recognition

source1: I had to face the possibility I might die in prison.
For the first time, I was allowed to think, to build, to be more than just another person serving time.
source2: I thought my life was over.
That vulnerability knocked down walls. I learned that sharing my story heals.
source3: Addiction is merely a symptom of complete spiritual brokenness.
